critique.summary: The article discusses common Java performance anti-patterns that can significantly degrade application speed and memory usage. By identifying and fixing these issues, such as inefficient string concatenation, excessive stream usage, and improper synchronization, the author demonstrates substantial performance improvements in a Java order-processing app.
mainPoints: String concatenation in loops causes O(n²) copying due to String immutability; using StringBuilder improves performance.
Using streams inside loops can cause accidental O(n²) complexity; replacing with single-pass aggregation reduces CPU usage.
String.format() is slow in hot paths because it parses the format string every call; prefer StringBuilder or partial formatting.
Autoboxing in tight loops creates many temporary wrapper objects, increasing heap usage and GC pressure; use primitives instead.
Using exceptions for control flow is costly due to stack trace generation; pre-validate inputs to avoid frequent exceptions.
Broad synchronization locks cause contention bottlenecks; use ConcurrentHashMap and LongAdder for high concurrency.
Repeated creation of expensive reusable objects like ObjectMapper or DateTimeFormatter hurts performance; reuse instances.
Virtual thread pinning in JDK 21–23 occurs when synchronized blocks with blocking I/O prevent unmounting carrier threads; use ReentrantLock or upgrade to JDK 24 to mitigate.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are accurate and align with widely recognized Java performance best practices and benchmarks. The examples and fixes correspond to known issues documented by reputable sources such as BellSoft, Baeldung, and real-world production experiences from companies like Netflix. The discussion of virtual thread pinning and JDK versions is consistent with official Java Enhancement Proposals and release notes. Overall, the content is factually reliable and supported by empirical evidence.
considerations: The article focuses on micro-optimizations and may not address higher-level architectural or algorithmic improvements that could yield larger gains.
Performance impacts can vary depending on workload, JVM version, and hardware; profiling is essential to identify actual bottlenecks.
Some fixes, like replacing synchronized with ReentrantLock, may have trade-offs in complexity or semantics that need careful consideration.
The article does not discuss the impact of Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler optimizations or garbage collector tuning, which also affect performance.
The examples assume familiarity with Java concurrency and profiling tools, which may limit accessibility for beginners.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Java's performance characteristics, focusing on common pitfalls like string handling, autoboxing, and exception usage. Commenters highlight both language design issues and typical coding practices that impact speed, with some suggesting alternative languages for better control and performance.

comments.author: spankalee
comments.text:
Avoiding Java's string footguns is an interesting problem in programming languages design.

The String.format() problem is most immediately a bad compiler and bad implementation, IMO. It's not difficult to special-case literal strings as the first argument, do parsing at compile time, and pass in a structured representation. The method could also do runtime caching. Even a very small LRU cache would fix a lot of common cases. At the very least they should let you make a formatter from a specific format string and reuse it, like you can with regexes, to explicitly opt into better performance.

But ultimately the string templates proposal should come back and fix this at the language level. Better syntax and guaranteed compile-time construction of the template. The language should help the developer do the fast thing.

String concatenation is a little trickier. In a JIT'ed language you have a lot of options for making a hierarchy of string implementations that optimize different usage patterns, and still be fast - and what you really want for concatenation is a RopeString, like JS VMs have, that simply references the other strings. The issue is that you don't want virtual calls for hot-path string method calls.

Java chose a single final class so all calls are direct. But they should have been able to have a very small sealed class hierarchy where most methods are final and directly callable, and the virtual methods for accessing storage are devirtualized in optimized methods that only ever see one or two classes through a call site.

To me, that's a small complexity cost to make common string patterns fast, instead of requiring StringBuilder.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and technically sound analysis of Java string performance issues, proposing realistic improvements at both the compiler and language level.

comments.author: liampulles
comments.text:
Understanding algorithmic complexity (in particular, avoiding rework in loops), is useful in any language, and is sage advice.

In practice though, for most enterprise web services, a lot of real world performance comes down to how efficiently you are calling external services (including the database). Just converting a loop of queries into bulk ones can help loads (and then tweaking the query to make good use of indexes, doing upserts, removing unneeded data, etc.)

I'm hopeful that improvements in LLMs mean we can ditch ORMs (under the guise that they are quicker to write queries and the inbetween mapping code with) and instead make good use of SQL to harness the powers that modern databases provide.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly emphasizes that algorithmic efficiency and database query optimization often have a greater impact on performance than language-specific micro-optimizations.

comments.author: Okx
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies a limitation in the Java standard library's number parsing and highlights the inefficiency of exception handling in this context.

comments.author: cogman10
comments.analysis: The suggestion to replace a map with an array for fixed-size indexing is a well-known optimization that reduces overhead and improves speed in this scenario.

comments.author: cmovq
comments.text:
When you're using a programming language that naturally steers you to write slow code you can't only blame the programmer.

I was listening to someone say they write fast code in Java by avoiding allocations with a PoolAllocator that would "cache" small objects with poolAllocator.alloc(), poolAllocator.release(). So just manual memory management with extra steps. At that point why not use a better language for the task?
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully points out that language design influences code performance and that sometimes choosing a different language is more practical than complex workarounds.

comments.author: wood_spirit
comments.text:
A subject close to my heart, I write a lot of heavily optimised code including a lot of hot data pipelines in Java.

And aside from algorithms, it usually comes down to avoiding memory allocations.

I have my go-to zero-alloc grpc and parquet and json and time libs etc and they make everything fast.

It’s mostly how idiomatic Java uses objects for everything that makes it slow overall.

But eventually after making a JVM app that keeps data in something like data frames etc and feels a long way from J2EE beans you can finally bump up against the limits that only c/c++/rust/etc can get you past.
comments.analysis: The comment provides practical insight into Java performance, emphasizing memory allocation overhead and the benefits of specialized libraries and data structures.

comments.author: kpw94
comments.text:
The Autoboxing example imo is a case of "Java isn't so fast". Why can't this be optimized behind the scenes by the compiler ?

Rest of advice is great: things compilers can't really catch but a good code reviewer should point out.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies autoboxing as a performance pitfall and suggests that compiler improvements and code reviews are necessary to mitigate it.

comments.author: kyrra
comments.text:
First request latency also can really suck in Java before hotpathed code gets through the C2 compiler. You can warm up hotpaths by running that code during startup, but it's really annoying having to do that. Using C++, Go, or Rust gets you around that problem without having to jump through the hoops of code path warmup.

I wish Java had a proper compiler.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately describes JVM warmup latency and contrasts it with languages that compile ahead of time, which have more consistent startup performance.

comments.author: jerf
comments.text: Any non-trivial program that has never had an optimizer run on it has a minimal-effort 50+% speedup in it.
comments.analysis: This is a general but true statement about the impact of compiler optimizations on performance, though it lacks specific context.

comments.author: zvqcMMV6Zcr
comments.text:
> Exceptions for Control Flow

This one is so prevalent that JVM has an optimization where it gives up on filling stack for exception, if it was thrown over and over in exact same place.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a factual JVM optimization detail that mitigates the cost of exceptions used for control flow.

comments.author: bearjaws
comments.text:
JavaScript can be fast too, it's just the ecosystem and decisions devs make that slow it down.

Same for Java, I have yet to in my entire career see enterprise Java be performant and not memory intensive.

At the end of the day, if you care about performance at the app layer, you will use a language better suited to that.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully compares Java and JavaScript performance issues as often stemming from ecosystem and usage patterns rather than inherent language speed.

comments.author: koakuma-chan
comments.text: As much as I love Java, everybody should just be using Rust. That way you are actually in control, know what's going on, etc. Another reason specifically against Java is that the tooling, both Maven and Gradle, still stucks.
comments.analysis: While somewhat opinionated, the comment correctly notes Rust's advantages in control and performance and points out common frustrations with Java tooling.

comments.author: victor106
comments.text:
this is great, so practical!!!

any other resources like this?
comments.analysis: This comment expresses enthusiasm but adds no substantive content or analysis.

comments.author: tripple6
comments.text: Do good, don't do bad. Okay.
comments.analysis: This comment is a vague platitude without meaningful contribution to the discussion.
